title: Fail clean: total error boundary and ERROR mapping
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). The operational boundary where a scheduler-driven monitor lives or dies; this epic is the release gate. Includes end-to-end tests of the ERROR paths (currently none reach exit 3).

P0 -- The CLI error boundary catches only ValueError, but load_config and the engine also raise FileNotFoundError, KeyError, and TypeError on ordinary mistakes: run -c missing.yaml gives a FileNotFoundError traceback; a check missing object: raises KeyError (config.py:136); expect: 5 raises TypeError (checks.py:159); a source missing type: raises KeyError. An unhandled traceback exits with code 1, which the documented 0-3 contract defines as WARN -- so a monitoring wrapper treats a crashed run as 'soft-bound violations only'. Introduce a ConfigError raised by load_config for all validation/parse failures (wrap read_text, YAML parse, and field access); catch (ConfigError, OSError, yaml.YAMLError) at each command boundary; add a last-resort handler in main() so nothing exits 1 by accident; give config errors a distinct exit code (currently 2, colliding with FAIL).

P0 -- Unreachable source at connect time crashes the run instead of mapping to ERROR (spec 13). A connection that fails at adapter construction (create_adapter in runner.py's per-source comprehension; SqlAlchemyAdapter.__init__ and dbsql.connect connect eagerly) gives a raw traceback, exit 1, no digest/JSON, no other sources evaluated, nothing persisted, and leaked adapters. Wrap per-source adapter creation; synthesize ERROR results (with the exception text) for every check on a failed source; continue other sources; only build adapters for sources that have checks.

P1 -- A worker-thread exception aborts the whole run. compile_metric_sql is called OUTSIDE the try/except in _evaluate_check (engine.py:123), so a metric typo (never validated at load) raises ValueError inside a ThreadPoolExecutor worker; pool.map re-raises it in the main thread, the run dies, nothing is persisted, and other sources' completed work is discarded. Wrap the whole per-check body so any exception becomes a Status.ERROR Result (upstream, validate metric names -- see the config epic).

P2 -- Adapter close paths are not exception-safe: in run_and_persist's finally, if the first adapter.close() raises, the rest leak; in SqlAlchemyAdapter.close, a failing _conn.close() skips engine.dispose(). Wrap each close in try/except; use try/finally inside close().

P2 -- Error messages are rough: a missing config file gives a raw traceback; the duplicate check_id error is an opaque hash naming neither colliding check (two checks differing only in where: collide by design, spec 8.2, with no hint toward the explicit id: remedy); a mistyped --store silently creates an empty database and reports 'no observations found'. Name both colliding checks (source/object/metric/column/key) plus the id: remedy; warn when history opens a store file it just created.
close_reason: Fail-clean P0 implemented: ConfigError boundary (exit 3, last-resort main() guard), per-source connect failures map to ERROR without aborting the run, an evaluate_check safety net so any escaped exception becomes ERROR (no worker crash), exception-safe adapter closes, and a diagnosable duplicate-check_id message. 561 tests (23 new, each RED-then-GREEN).

title: Wire freshness_source into the engine for describe_history/describe_detail freshness
description: The Check model has no freshness_source field, so a freshness check's freshness_source: describe_history/describe_detail (proposed by the configurator for Databricks tables, emitted by the CLI) is dropped at config load; compile_metric_sql always emits SELECT MAX(column) and _evaluate_freshness always runs it. The Databricks adapter now provides the metadata origins (describe_history_last_modified via DESCRIBE HISTORY filtered to data operations; describe().last_modified via DESCRIBE DETAIL) plus a validate_freshness_source capability/view gate, but nothing calls them. Wire it end-to-end: (1) add a freshness_source field to Check defaulting to 'column' and parse it in config; (2) config validation gates freshness_source on the source's dialect capability and rejects the DESCRIBE forms on views, and makes column optional for the DESCRIBE forms while still requiring it for 'column'; (3) _evaluate_freshness dispatches: 'column' uses MAX(col) SQL as today, 'describe_history' calls adapter.describe_history_last_modified(obj), 'describe_detail' uses adapter.describe(obj).last_modified, then computes lag against the result. Makes the Databricks metadata-freshness path and the adapter capabilities reachable.
close_reason: Wired freshness_source end-to-end: Check field + config parsing, static dialect-capability validation (dialect_for_type + validate_freshness_source), run-time view check, and _evaluate_freshness dispatch to describe_detail/describe_history. 533 tests. Real-Databricks is_view population is a small follow-up: df-y3f.

title: Prune redundant full-screen TUI snapshots (empty-state, history-trend)
description:
Snapshot audit (behavioral-first guideline): two pre-existing full-screen snapshots add little visual coverage over existing behavioral tests and are candidates to prune, keeping the behavioral coverage.

- test_home_dashboard_shows_empty_state_with_no_checks: a trivial screen (centered 'no checks' message, grid hidden). Behavior is asserted by test_home_shows_empty_state_when_no_checks_are_configured / test_home_hides_empty_state_once_checks_exist. Same redundant+low-visual pattern as the removed missing-secrets-banner snapshot.
- test_history_screen_shows_trend: the colorized history component is exhaustively behaviorally tested in test_tui_screens.py (recolor each row status, fail/error stay distinct, value-column alignment including skipped, expected/error shown). The full-screen snapshot's marginal visual value over those is low.

Keep the high-visual-value snapshots: mixed-statuses (status color-coding across every status + legend), object-detail (cursor-row readability -- caught the black-text bug), configure-initial-layout (widget-dense, clipping risk). Defensible-keep: report failures/warnings (digest colorization), configure new-source form (form layout). Not urgent; do on a dedicated test-hygiene branch, not bundled into a feature PR.
close_reason: Pruned history-trend and empty-state snapshots; behavioral coverage retained; kept the visual-value snapshots

title: TUI run and propose feedback
description:
Umbrella for making the two database-touching TUI actions -- Run and Propose -- responsive and legible. Today both give near-zero feedback.

Propose (configure.py:135-144, 323-395) runs create_adapter + check_object_exists + describe synchronously in the button handler with no worker, so a slow or unreachable source freezes the whole app for up to the connection timeout with no spinner and no disabled button. Run (app.py:112-120) starts a thread worker but surfaces no in-progress state and no success toast (only the error path notifies, app.py:138-144), and the per-check progress plumbing the CLI uses (report.progress_reporter / on_result, wired at cli.py:196-199) is never passed (app.py:120).

This is the part of the TUI that most reads as unfinished in daily use. Children cover the Propose worker, a run-in-progress indicator plus a success toast, per-check progress, refreshing the topmost pushed screen on run completion, and unifying Configure's split error surfacing.
close_reason: All five run/propose-feedback children delivered in one PR

title: Run Propose in a worker thread with a visible busy state
description:
ConfigureScreen._propose (configure.py:135-144, 323-395) runs create_adapter + check_object_exists + describe synchronously in the button handler with no @work decorator, so Propose against a slow or unreachable source freezes the entire app for up to the source timeout with no spinner, no disabled button, and no feedback.

Move the introspection into a thread worker like the Run action (app.py:116-120): disable Propose and show an in-progress indicator while it runs, then mount the proposal on completion and re-enable. This is the worst responsiveness defect in the TUI.
close_reason: Run/propose feedback: Propose worker + busy state, run progress + success toast, refresh topmost screen, unified error surfacing

title: Show run-in-progress state and a success toast with status counts
description:
action_run_checks starts a thread worker (app.py:112-120) but nothing surfaces WorkerState.RUNNING -- no spinner, no status line -- and success is silent (the dashboard refreshes at app.py:135-137 but only the error path notifies, app.py:138-144).

Add a visible in-progress indicator while a run is active and, on success, a toast summarizing counts (e.g. "N ok, M failed, K unreachable"). Also handle the double-press case: a second r silently cancels the first exclusive worker with no notice.
close_reason: Run/propose feedback: Propose worker + busy state, run progress + success toast, refresh topmost screen, unified error surfacing

title: Wire per-check progress into the TUI run
description:
run_and_persist accepts the same on_result / progress callback the CLI uses (report.progress_reporter, wired at cli.py:196-199), but the TUI calls it with no callback (app.py:120).

Feed per-check progress into the run-in-progress indicator so a long run shows which checks have completed rather than a single opaque spinner. Depends on the in-progress indicator existing.
close_reason: Run/propose feedback: Propose worker + busy state, run progress + success toast, refresh topmost screen, unified error surfacing

title: Refresh the topmost pushed screen when a run completes
description:
on_worker_state_changed refreshes the Home grid (app.py:135-137), but ObjectDetail populates once in on_mount (screens.py:132-139) and Report renders once in compose, so pressing r while viewing a failing object or the report does nothing visible until esc + re-enter.

Refresh whichever screen is on top when a run finishes so the natural "run and watch it recover" gesture works.
close_reason: Run/propose feedback: Propose worker + busy state, run progress + success toast, refresh topmost screen, unified error surfacing

title: Unify Configure error surfacing
description: The Configure screen surfaces errors two ways: Save uses toasts (configure.py:304, 311) while Propose writes unstyled inline text into #proposal-text (configure.py:331, 338, 344, 351). Pick one channel for user-facing errors on this screen and apply it consistently.
close_reason: Run/propose feedback: Propose worker + busy state, run progress + success toast, refresh topmost screen, unified error surfacing

title: Reporting contract: spec-shape JSON, --only, and progress
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e).

P1 -- --json omits most of the spec's stable-contract envelope (spec 10.1). Implemented as {status, results}; spec is {status, run_id, started_at, finished_at, counts, results}, each result carrying check_id, source, object, metric, label, tier, status, value, value_text, expected, observed, error, samples. Today results lack tier, value_text, observed, and diff; the schema fingerprint rides in value instead of value_text (inconsistent with the store's own split); run_id and timestamps exist in the store but are never threaded to the renderer. Shipping a wrong 'stable contract' guarantees a breaking change later. Thread run metadata (run_id or null under --no-store, started/finished timestamps) and counts into render_json; add tier (from column/key presence), split value/value_text per the store's rule, include observed and diff; fix the Quickstart JSON example (it also omits the label field the code emits).

P2 -- run has no --only SOURCE and no progress bar / --no-progress, both in the spec (sections 9 and 10.1). rich is declared specifically for progress yet is only used in the TUI, and the Quickstart says --json 'suppresses the progress bar' that does not exist. --only also matters operationally (exclude a down source). Filter checks and adapter construction by --only; add a rich progress bar suppressed under --json/--no-progress/non-TTY.
close_reason: Reporting contract: --json now emits the stable envelope {status, run_id, started_at, finished_at, counts, results} with each result carrying tier, split value/value_text, observed, and diff (split_value moved to engine.py, shared by store and report); RunResult carries run_id/started_at/finished_at from run_and_persist; --only SOURCE filters checks before adapters are built (excluded adapters never constructed) with a clean unknown-source error; a rich progress bar shows M-of-N, suppressed under --json/--no-progress/non-TTY; the quickstart JSON example matches the emitted shape. 690 tests.

title: Baseline scaffold: connection parser, source adapters, two primitives, builtins, YAML config, reporting, engine tests
description:
Package skeleton; connection URL parser (usql/go-mssqldb -> SqlServerParams); SQL Server (pymssql) and Databricks (databricks-sql-connector) adapters behind the scalar/rows/close protocol; the two primitives (metric+expectation, assertion); builtins row_count, null_rate, duplicate_count, sum/avg/min/max, freshness; YAML config with ${VAR} env interpolation; rich progress + plain-text digest + JSON output; exit codes; sqlite-backed engine tests.
Accept: pytest green; digest renders as in dbfresh.md section 9. Spec: dbfresh.md section 13.
close_reason: Superseded by the E1-E11 epic plan in dbfresh.md

title: Dashboard rolls a SKIPPED-only node up to OK instead of SKIPPED
description: worst_status maps rank 0 back to OK, and SKIPPED shares rank 0 with OK, so worst_status({SKIPPED}) returns OK. The TUI dashboard reuses worst_status for node rollups (_worst_or_unknown in tui/dashboard.py), so a source/object/column whose only known child is SKIPPED displays [OK] even though the leaf itself shows [SKIPPED] — a green all-clear for a check that was intentionally not run. Exit-code aggregation correctly treats SKIPPED as 0, so the fix belongs in the dashboard rollup, not in worst_status: when all known children are SKIPPED, roll the node up to SKIPPED. This will change the TUI snapshot baseline for the affected column.
close_reason: Dashboard rollup returns SKIPPED when all known children are SKIPPED; worst_status unchanged; one baseline regenerated.

title: Business calendar: weekday/holiday overrides, business-time freshness, skip_off_schedule
description: calendar.py with holidays package; by_weekday/on_holiday selection; calendar: business freshness; skip_off_schedule to SKIPPED. See dbfresh.md epic E4.
close_reason: Business calendar (holidays), by_weekday/on_holiday overrides, business-time freshness, and skip_off_schedule implemented.

title: Remove spelled-out 'spec section N' references from code and tests
description: The earlier cleanup removed $-sigil section references but missed spelled-out ones. Six remain: src/dbfresh/calendar.py (module docstring 'spec section 7', class 'spec section 7.1', method 'spec section 7.3'), src/dbfresh/store.py (module 'spec section 8'), src/dbfresh/config.py (StoreConfig 'spec section 8.1'), tests/test_configurator_offers.py (module 'spec section 11.2'). Replace each with a plain description of what the code does, no section pointer. These are process artifacts that should not live in code/tests per the project convention.
close_reason: Removed the six spelled-out 'spec section N' references the earlier sigil-based cleanup missed (calendar.py module/class/method, store.py module, config.py StoreConfig, test_configurator_offers.py module); each docstring now describes the code plainly.

title: Config schema completeness and validation
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). Implement or excise every documented-but-missing config field, and bring validation up to spec 13 (runs before any connection, reports all problems at once). Validating metric names here also removes the worker-crash in the fail-clean epic.

P1 -- Source timezone: and timeout: are specified and documented but unimplemented. Any extra source param raises TypeError at adapter construction, so the spec's and docs/configuration.md's own example config cannot run. And engine._to_aware_utc unconditionally assumes naive timestamps are UTC, so freshness lag for a non-UTC source (SQL Server datetime columns are typically naive) is silently off by the offset -- the exact class of silent wrongness the tool exists to catch. Accept timezone:/timeout:, thread the source timezone into freshness evaluation, pass timeout to the drivers, reject genuinely unknown source params with a clean validation error.

P1 -- assert_sql: is documented in three docs pages and referenced by spec 8.2/15 but does not exist; a check with assert_sql parses as metric-less and crashes with 'unsupported metric: None'. Implement raw assertion queries (compile to the dialect-capped fetch, persist the violation count, include the normalized text in check_id) or excise it from docs and make a no-metric-no-assert check a validation error.

P1 -- Validation is far short of spec: metric typos (rowcount) pass then crash mid-run; null_rate without column compiles the literal 'None' into SQL; duplicate_count without key likewise; unknown check-block keys are ignored (typos like colum:); an invalid severity behaves as error; a metric check with no expect: is always-OK; by_weekday/on_holiday on assertions are ignored; max_lag on non-freshness metrics is ignored; errors raise on the first problem instead of accumulating. Validate metric names against registry.METRICS (it exists and is parity-tested), require the discriminating field per metric, require an expectation on metric checks, whitelist check-block keys, validate severity, restrict max_lag to freshness, and report all errors at once.

P1 -- skip_on_holiday alias (spec 7.4, docs/calendar.md) is silently ignored; a documented safety field is a no-op, so the check still runs on holidays. Accept the alias or remove it from spec and docs.

P3 -- config.example.yaml is in the spec's architecture layout but does not exist; add a copyable example file.
close_reason: Config schema completeness and validation. Up-front validation now accumulates and reports every problem at once (registry-checked metric names, required discriminating field, required expectation, whitelisted check keys, valid severity, max_lag only on freshness, plus the existing source-ref / freshness-capability / duplicate-id / calendar checks). Sources take first-class timezone/timeout and reject genuinely-unknown params via adapter-signature introspection. Naive freshness timestamps are interpreted in the source timezone. skip_on_holiday alias honored. assert_sql implemented. config.example.yaml added. 658 tests.

title: TUI Configure: per-column offered checks and per-check trim
description: Deferred from df-34m. The CLI add wizard offers per-column checks (the 'Offered for X: null_rate, sum, ...' step) and lets the user trim proposed checks; the TUI Configure screen has neither, so the two authoring surfaces are not at parity. Add a per-column offered-checks selection and a per-check trim UI to the Configure screen. These are more substantial Textual UI additions (widgets plus wiring) than the rest of df-34m, hence deferred.
close_reason: Added per-column offered-checks selection and per-check trim to the TUI Configure screen, reaching parity with the CLI add wizard on which checks to include and dropping proposed ones. Reuses offered_column_checks/build_offered_check/append_checks; Pilot tests plus a regenerated Configure snapshot. Offered-check threshold customization deferred to df-xwo.

title: Reduce friction in discovering a config's required environment variables
description:
Onboarding: make it easier to learn which environment variables a config needs before running. Two candidate approaches -- pick one or neither.

(a) A 'dbfresh env -c config.yaml' command that scans the raw config YAML for ${VAR} references before interpolation and lists, per variable, the source/field(s) it feeds and whether it is set after the normal .env load. A preflight for 'is my environment ready'. If built: table output with secrets never printed (set/unset only); decide exit code (informational 0 vs a gate that fails when any referenced var is unset); decide whether to scan included config files.

(b) Leaner alternative: interpolate_env in config.py raises on the FIRST undefined ${VAR}, so a config missing three variables costs three fix-run cycles. Make that error accumulate and report ALL missing variables at once, matching the batch config validation already in _validate_sources / _validate_checks. Fixes the real 'one error at a time' friction inside the normal load, no new command surface.

Recommendation: (b). It addresses the actual pain with less surface; (a) mostly re-lists ${VAR}s the author already wrote and cannot infer value shape (the Environment & secrets docs page covers shape). Split from df-l1d; undecided pending maintainer input.

title: Config composition: include globs, path resolution, cross-file duplicate detection
description: include globs (root-only), included-file constraints, config-relative path resolution, cross-file duplicate check_id, deterministic load order. See dbfresh.md epic E6.
close_reason: Config composition: include globs (root-only, config-dir-relative, deterministic order), included-file constraints, cross-file duplicate check_id detection, empty-glob error; defaults merging generalized to severity/calendar/where/allow_empty/skip_off_schedule. Verified end-to-end.

title: Archive the original spec as docs/original-specification.md
description: Once the v1-review follow-ups are all merged, archive dbfresh.md (the original implementation specification) as docs/original-specification.md -- a historical record of where the project started, superseded by the shipped code and the mkdocs docs site. Move it there with a short header noting it is the original spec, and update the CLAUDE.md Key Files reference. Deferred until the follow-ups that still read dbfresh.md for design are complete, so their references do not break mid-flight.
close_reason: Archived the original implementation spec as docs/original-specification.md with a historical-document header; updated the README, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and the stray sqlserver.py docstring pointer to the new path; added it to the mkdocs nav. Strict docs build and just check green.

title: Engine and store mechanics: dedupe, evaluation-time persistence, WAL
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e).

P2 -- Engine boilerplate is duplicated five times over: the try/except returning a Status.ERROR Result (in _evaluate_check, _evaluate_assertion, _evaluate_freshness, _evaluate_schema, _evaluate_vs_previous), the WARN/FAIL severity ternary, and twelve near-identical Result(object=..., metric=..., source=...) constructions. engine.py is the largest module at 532 lines and every new metric adds another copy. Extract _error_result(check, exc, ...), _verdict(check, passed), and a _result(check, ...) factory; the module drops about 80 lines and new evaluators become uniform.

P2 -- run_and_persist threads now into evaluation but NOT into start_run/record_observation, which stamp wall-clock time -- evaluation and its recorded observation can disagree (matters for last_same_weekday's 6-day floor) and frozen-clock persistence testing is impossible. Also start_run is called AFTER all checks complete, so started_at is approximately finished_at and both post-date the run, observations get persist-time observed_at, and a mid-run crash persists nothing. Thread now (started_at/observed_at) through persistence; start the run row and capture git_sha before evaluation; stamp results with their evaluation time.

P2 -- record_observation commits per row; a 200-check run is 200 fsyncs. Wrap the persist loop in one transaction.

P2 -- Store durability/concurrency posture is implicit: one sqlite3 connection, check_same_thread=False, no lock, shared across worker threads -- safe today ONLY by an unenforced invariant (workers read-only during evaluation, all writes post-run on one thread) that moving start_run earlier would break. No WAL mode or busy_timeout, so two overlapping dbfresh run processes (cron overlap) hit 'database is locked'. Add PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL and PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000 at open; add a threading.Lock around store methods or an explicit asserted read-only-during-run invariant.
close_reason: Engine Result plumbing deduped (_result/_error_result/_verdict, byte-exact); run row opened before evaluation with started_at=now and observations stamped observed_at=now (evaluation time); observations written in one transaction per run via record_observations; store opens with PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL + busy_timeout for cross-process cron overlap; the single-writer-during-evaluation invariant is documented. 608 tests.

title: Release and publish workflow (PyPI)
description: GitHub Actions release workflow to build and publish the wheel/sdist on tag, enabling the Quickstart's uv tool install / pipx install.

title: TUI: 'days since last issue' streak column on the status grid
description: Follow-on to df-bg6 (the source/check-by-day status grid), deferred for later -- not urgent. Idea from discussing df-bg6: an additional leading column (alongside 'overall' and the day-by-day trend cells) showing how many days have elapsed since that row's (object's or check's) last non-OK observation -- an uptime-style streak counter. Needs df-bg6's grid to exist first. Technical wrinkle: this can't just read off the grid's fixed 7-day trend window -- a healthy check with no recent failure needs to look back further than 7 days to find its last issue, which means its own store query beyond what the trend columns already fetch. Also bounded by retention: Store.prune()/retain_days means observations eventually age out, so a check with no failure in the retained window should read as 'no issue in recorded history' (or similar), not a false 'never failed' claim beyond what's actually knowable.

title: CLI add wizard: choose source type from supported types instead of free-text
description: The TUI Configure new-source form now picks the source type from a Select of factory.supported_types() (df-el0). The CLI 'dbfresh add' wizard's _select_source (cli.py) still free-types the type at a text prompt -- same typo-to-'unknown source type'-at-connect failure. Offer/validate against factory.supported_types() there too (a numbered menu or a validated prompt). Small CLI/TUI parity fix.
close_reason: CLI _select_source now presents supported_types() as a numbered menu (via _prompt_index) instead of free-text; parity with the TUI dropdown; tests updated + validation test added

title: TUI/CLI: format check values by metric type instead of raw floats
description:
GitHub issue #34. render_digest/render_history (report.py) and the TUI's Report/History screens (which reuse those same render functions) print every observed value as a bare Python scalar with no metric-aware formatting. Settled design:

Per-check block restructure: lead each failing check's digest block with the human summary (result.expected, already Expectation.describe() output -- e.g. 'max_lag 24h' or 'vs_previous(previous) ratio [0.5, 2.0]') on its own/header line, instead of sharing a line with 'observed'; give the formatted observed value its own line.

Per-metric formatting rules (display-only, no change to stored precision):
- freshness -> human duration, two significant units ('5d 9h', '45m', '12s')
- freshness only, additionally: append the absolute last-update timestamp in parens, e.g. 'observed: 5d 9h stale (last update: 2026-07-09T03:42:14-04:00)'. Reconstructed at render time as t0 = reference_time - timedelta(seconds=lag_value) -- exact, not approximate, because runner.py resolves now once per run and reuses that same value both for every check's lag computation and as the persisted observed_at for that run's observations. reference_time is run.started_at for the live post-run digest, row['observed_at'] for each history row. No schema change, no new Result field needed. Reuse format_timestamp() (the same ISO-8601 helper already used for the report header and history rows) rather than a second date style.
- duplicate_count -> plain integer
- row_count -> plain integer too (not named in the issue, but same COUNT(*)-is-always-integral situation as duplicate_count; currently prints '60.0')
- null_rate -> percentage, 1 decimal ('4.0%')
- sum / avg / min / max -> rounded to 2 decimals

Also settled: display_timezone() (report.py) currently falls back to None (rendered as UTC/Z) whenever no business calendar is configured -- the common case. Change that fallback to the local system timezone instead of UTC, so the report header, history rows, and the new freshness parenthetical are all local by default and consistent with each other, still ISO 8601 (format_timestamp already renders a numeric +HH:MM/-HH:MM offset for any non-zero-offset tz, not just Z). A configured business calendar's zone still wins, unchanged. No-op on a server already running in UTC.

Also asked for: a one-line human-readable expectation summary per check -- resolved above as leading each block with result.expected.
close_reason: Resolved in PR #36 (merged to main): metric-aware value formatting in report.py, plus display_timezone() defaulting to local system time.

title: Shared config: secrets onboarding and maintenance
description:
The versioned-config-shared-via-git workflow: a colleague drops in a git-tracked config.yaml plus their own .env, and pulls config updates over time.

Already supported (verified): dbfresh defaults to ./config.yaml and takes -c PATH (cli.py:130); it auto-loads a .env beside the config for every config-reading command including ui (cli.py:584, python-dotenv), with real env vars taking precedence; check_id hashes identity, not the expectation (checks.py), so a threshold edit preserves history across the team; and because definitions are git-tracked while observations are per-machine local SQLite, a pulled new check simply runs and establishes its own local baseline on the next run (schema unchanged passes and baselines on first run; vs_previous builds forward).

Gaps this epic closes: dbfresh ui hard-errors to stderr and never launches when a ${VAR} secret is unset (inconsistent with df-12w's friendly empty-config launch); no way to emit an .env.example of the vars a config needs; no signal about checks added since a colleague last ran; and no config discovery beyond ./config.yaml. Related: df-j5x.2 (a manual config-reload binding) covers picking up a pulled config without restarting the TUI.

title: Launch the TUI with a missing-secrets banner instead of erroring out
description:
When a config that exists references ${VAR} secrets that aren't set, dbfresh ui currently raises ConfigError, prints to stderr via _report_config_error, and never launches (load_config collects the missing vars then raises; cli.py _ui_command). Instead, launch the TUI and show a prominent banner naming the missing secrets and where to set them (a .env beside config.yaml, or the environment).

Needs a tolerant load mode that collects the missing ${VAR} and leaves them literal rather than raising (interpolate_env already supports a `missing` set; load_config is what raises when it's non-empty). Sources whose params still hold a literal ${VAR} come back ERROR on a run, which df-m2k now renders as clean status/toasts rather than a crash. This mirrors df-12w's friendly empty-config launch for the 'config present but not fully set up' case. Chosen UX (launch-and-notify) over a plain better-error message.
close_reason: Missing-secrets banner: ui-only tolerant load, warning-yellow banner, reload also tolerant

title: Generate an .env.example from a config's ${VAR} references
description: A config already knows every ${VAR} it references -- interpolate_env collects them and the undefined-var error already names them. Add a way to emit an .env.example listing exactly those variable names (empty values), so a colleague sharing the repo knows precisely what to set. E.g. a `dbfresh env-template` command or a flag. Companion to the versioned-config workflow: commit config.yaml + .env.example, gitignore .env, colleagues copy and fill.

title: Surface checks added since a colleague last ran
description: After pulling an updated config, new checks appear on Home as 'never observed' and quietly run on the next run -- a colleague gets no signal about what was added. Surface a count/hint (e.g. 'N checks not yet run on this machine') so pulled additions are noticeable. Observations are per-machine, so 'in config with no local observation' is the natural signal.

title: Config discovery: walk up for config.yaml, and a DBFRESH_CONFIG env var
description: dbfresh only looks at ./config.yaml (or -c PATH) -- no walk-up discovery and no DBFRESH_CONFIG env var (unlike DBFRESH_STORE). Running dbfresh ui from a subdirectory of the repo misses the root config.yaml and instead shows df-12w's empty-config TUI, which misleads someone who does have a config a level up. Add walk-up discovery and/or a DBFRESH_CONFIG env var.

title: Document the versioned-config + .env sharing workflow
description: Document the recommended team workflow on the docs site: commit config.yaml and an .env.example, gitignore .env; each colleague copies .env.example to .env and fills their secrets; pulling config updates picks up new checks (which run and establish local baselines) with history preserved across threshold edits. Write once the capabilities above land; do not pre-write.

title: Consider reclaiming disk after a TUI prune (VACUUM/compact)
close_reason: Decided against auto-VACUUM/compact and against surfacing it in the UI; the prune-does-not-reclaim-disk rationale is documented in docs/history.md instead

comments.author: dchud
comments.text: StoreScreen's Prune now deletes rows but does not VACUUM, so the displayed size_bytes may not drop after a prune even as the observation count falls -- SQLite keeps freed pages in the file for reuse. Matches the CLI 'dbfresh prune' (also no VACUUM), so not a regression, but a store-visibility screen makes the unchanged size more noticeable. Decision needed: auto-VACUUM after prune (rewrites the whole file, can be slow, needs no other writers) vs a separate explicit Compact action vs leaving footprint-visibility as-is. Low priority.

title: Databricks describe() ordering breaks views
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e).

P1 -- DatabricksAdapter.describe() unconditionally runs DESCRIBE DETAIL (valid only for Delta tables) before computing is_view. On a view (a supported v1 target: Delta tables and views, spec 1) DESCRIBE DETAIL raises, so describe() raises: metric: schema on a Databricks view is ERROR every run; the freshness is_view guard surfaces a raw DESCRIBE error instead of the intended 'views must use a timestamp column' message; and the configurator cannot introspect views at all. Not live-testable here, but the call order is demonstrably wrong. Determine is_view first and skip DESCRIBE DETAIL for views (also wrap it defensively for non-Delta tables); add a contract-level test with a fake cursor that raises on DESCRIBE DETAIL for a view. While there, DESCRIBE HISTORY is fetched unbounded -- add a LIMIT.
close_reason: DatabricksAdapter.describe() computes is_view before last_modified and skips DESCRIBE DETAIL for views (which it is invalid against); _describe_detail_last_modified degrades to None on any failure (non-Delta tables); DESCRIBE HISTORY is bounded with LIMIT 20. 598 tests (+3).

title: Schema (shape) check: fingerprint, equals/unchanged, drift rendering
description: Fingerprint over describe() columns; equals (pinned) and unchanged (vs previous) expectations; drift rendering in the digest. See dbfresh.md epic E2.
close_reason: Schema (shape) check: describe()-based fingerprint, unchanged/equals, drift rendering, history-read threading. Verified end-to-end.

title: Consistency sweep: docs accuracy, layering, dead code, nits
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). Cross-cutting consistency, dead code, and docs accuracy.

P2 -- Docs stale or self-contradictory in load-bearing places: Quickstart and configuration.md say only run loads .env (false: all five config-reading commands do); configuration.md says the SQL Server/Databricks dialects are ahead of their live adapters (stale: the adapters shipped); history.md says vs_previous can combine with a static bound (contradicts the one-expectation-per-check rule); team-workflow.md says the TUI Configure asks which include file receives a block (it writes to target_files first entry without asking). One docs-accuracy sweep.

P2 -- validate_freshness_source and the describe_history_last_modified adapter method live in adapters/databricks.py but are imported/called by config.py and engine.py -- a generic function and a de-facto fifth adapter-protocol method sitting in an engine-specific module, against the engine-agnostic layering the spec repeats. Move validate_freshness_source (and the DESCRIBE-source set) into adapters/base.py; document describe_history_last_modified as the contract obligation of any dialect declaring describe_history.

P3 -- introspection_capabilities is declared, tested, and documented but read by nothing; the base Dialect and SqliteDialect do not declare it (sqlite exposes keys via reflection, so it should declare keys). Wire the configurator's degradation messaging to it or trim the docs claim and add a base-class default.

P3 -- structlog is a declared runtime dependency but is never imported; there is no logging at all. Drop the dependency or add minimal structured logging (run start/end, per-source connect, per-check ERROR) -- the latter would help debug the connect-failure class.

P3 -- Dead code with a per-run cost: BusinessCalendar.previous_business_day has zero production callers; ObjectInfo.approx_row_count is populated by the Postgres and SQL Server adapters at the cost of an extra catalog query on every describe() (sys.dm_db_partition_stats / pg_class) and is read by nothing -- every SQL Server schema check pays a query per run for an unused value. Wire a consumer or stop fetching.

P3 -- Catalog queries interpolate object names into quoted string literals (databricks table_name equals, mssql OBJECT_ID, pg to_regclass). Consistent with the trusted-config boundary (verified applied everywhere else; store queries are all parameterized), but a name containing a quote breaks the SQL, and the wizard/TUI feed user-typed object names into this path. Use the connector's parameter support or escape.

P3 -- Consistency nits: store.py's unparenthesized multi-except (valid PEP 758 but visually confusing -- parenthesize); adapters/base.py _split_object uses rpartition so db.schema.table on SQL Server mis-splits (document two-part support or split correctly); _ui_command parses the config twice (once to validate, once in the app); docsgen walks argparse privates (brittle, docs-only, add a comment); conftest patches a third-party attribute without a version guard; the copy-pasted seed/config test helpers should move to conftest fixtures.

P3 -- Repo scaffolding: GitHub issue templates (required by the E11 scaffolding scope) are absent -- only a PR template exists.

title: Make the TUI theme configurable (DBFRESH_THEME + document Textual themes)
description:
Textual bundles 21 built-in themes (all four Catppuccin flavors, dracula, gruvbox, nord, rose-pine x3, solarized x2, tokyo-night, monokai, flexoki, atom-one, ansi, textual-dark/light), and its command palette (Ctrl+P -> "Change theme") already switches among them live -- so a custom theme picker is not needed; the picker exists for free in every Textual app.

The wrinkle: dbfresh's own colors are hardcoded to Macchiato, so switching to another theme via the palette only half-applies today. dashboard._STATUS_STYLE uses literal Macchiato hexes, and app.tcss declares its custom $-variables (mantle/crust/overlay/blue/teal/...) as hardcoded Macchiato values (Textual's bundled theme does not expose those names). Standard widgets (Button, Input, DataTable, ...) retheme correctly; only the status glyph colors and dbfresh's custom-styled rules stay Macchiato.

Options, cheapest first:
- (a) Docs only: document the 21 themes and the Ctrl+P live switcher, with the caveat that status colors are Macchiato-tuned. Near-zero effort.
- (b) DBFRESH_THEME env var (default catppuccin-macchiato): set the initial App theme from an env var, so a user can pick a persistent default without the palette each launch. Small; same half-apply caveat.
- (c) Make the status colors and the custom app.tcss vars theme-aware -- derive them from the active theme's semantic colors (success/warning/error/...) rather than hardcoded hexes -- so any of the 21 themes fully applies, status glyphs included. This is the real work behind a clean multi-theme story.

Recommendation: (a)+(b) are cheap wins; (c) only if non-Macchiato themes should look fully coherent. Low priority -- follow-up to the df-b6o visual pass.

title: Editing a merge-key (<<:) check falls back to a full YAML round trip
description: A check that inherits its expect via a YAML merge key (<<: *anchor) has no own expect: line, so rewrite_check_expectation falls back to a full YAML round trip that expands all anchors and drops comments across the whole file. Semantically correct (the documented fallback for a shape the text splice cannot handle), but the blast radius is the entire file rather than the edited item. Acceptable as-is; recorded for awareness. Surfaced by the df-csv editing-lifecycle review; deferred.

Add optional value inputs so a user can set the threshold on a selected offered check from the TUI, matching the CLI wizard's prompts. Minor parity refinement; the two named capabilities (which offered checks to include, and per-check trim) already reached parity in df-p16.

title: Populate ObjectInfo.is_view in the real Databricks describe()
description: df-lsp added a run-time guard that rejects describe_history/describe_detail freshness on a view (validate_freshness_source with ObjectInfo.is_view), tested via a fake adapter. But the real DatabricksAdapter.describe() never sets is_view (it defaults False), so on a live Unity Catalog view a DESCRIBE-origin freshness check bypasses the friendly rejection and instead surfaces whatever error DESCRIBE HISTORY/DETAIL returns against a view. Populate is_view in DatabricksAdapter.describe() (e.g. from information_schema.tables.table_type = 'VIEW') so the guard fires with the clear 'views must use a timestamp column' message, and update the databricks adapter test fixtures for the added query.
close_reason: DatabricksAdapter.describe() now populates is_view from information_schema.tables (table_type == 'VIEW'); the run-time freshness guard rejects describe_history/describe_detail against a live view with the clear message. 5 new tests including the real-adapter path. 538 tests.

title: TUI: give the status grid more visual breathing room
description:
The Home/ObjectDetail DataTable (app.py, screens.py) is constructed with zero custom styling beyond cursor_type="row" -- default cell_padding=1, zebra_stripes=False. populate_grid (dashboard.py) never passes width= to add_column, so day columns auto-size to their 3-character "Mon"/"Tue" header text, and single-glyph centered cells end up crammed into narrow columns. Reported as visually dense/compact, hard on the eye.

Candidate remedies, feasibility-checked against Textual's actual DataTable API (not a final prescription -- needs visual iteration, i.e. actually looking at a snapshot, rather than guessing exact numbers blind):
- zebra_stripes=True (built-in constructor kwarg) for row scanability
- larger cell_padding (constructor kwarg, currently defaults to 1)
- explicit minimum width= on add_column for the day/overall columns so glyphs aren't jammed against their header text

Scope note: this is about spacing/padding within the compact grid format, not reverting to full status words -- that was the original problem df-bg6 fixed (the old tree burned too much vertical space for too little info). The goal is breathing room, not undoing the compactness.
close_reason: TUI visual pass: Catppuccin Macchiato theme, grid breathing room, Configure panels, snapshots re-baselined

title: Adding a SQL Server source fails unhelpfully when pymssql isn't installed
description:
pymssql is an optional extra (pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies] sqlserver = ["pymssql>=2.3.13"]), not a core dependency -- deliberate, so its native FreeTDS build is not forced on sqlite-only or Databricks-only installs, and the adapter imports fine without it (nothing imports pymssql at module level; SQLAlchemy only resolves the driver at connect time). The gap: adding a SQL Server source without the extra installed fails at connect/probe with a raw SQLAlchemy/driver error, and nothing tells the user to install the extra (reported: adding a SQL Server source in the TUI, pymssql missing, had to pip install it by hand).

Fix the experience without bloating the default install: catch the missing-driver failure at connect/probe time and surface an actionable hint -- SQL Server support needs the sqlserver extra, pip install "dbfresh[sqlserver]" (same shape for databricks) -- in both the TUI add-source probe and the CLI connection path. Confirm the extras are documented in the install/quickstart docs.

Open packaging call for the maintainer: alternatively promote pymssql to a default dependency (zero-friction for the primary SQL Server use case, at the cost of forcing the native driver build on every install including sqlite/Databricks-only users). Recommendation: keep it an extra and fix the error/hint.

title: TUI Configure: no way to add a new source
description:
ConfigureScreen.compose() (tui/configure.py) populates its source Select only from sorted(self._config.sources). At zero sources -- a brand-new project's config.yaml, before any source has ever been added -- the Select has no options and the screen is unusable end to end; there is no way to bootstrap a first source through the TUI at all.

Not strictly a regression: the pre-df-7p6 blank Input never supported creating a new source either -- _propose() just did self._config.sources.get(source_name) and reported "unknown source" for anything not already configured. Replacing that Input with a closed-list Select made the ceiling far more visible and, at zero sources, fully blocking rather than just failing after you type something.

The CLI wizard already has the real capability (cli.py's _select_source(), the new-source branch): prompts for type + connection params as freeform key=value lines, runs a mandatory probe_new_source() connection test before anything is written, and only then calls configurator.add_source(). All three of those (add_source, probe_connection, probe_new_source) are already front-end-agnostic in configurator.py -- this is a UI-surface gap in the TUI, not new backend work.

Proposed direction: a "+ new source" affordance on the Select (or falling straight through to a new-source form when self._config.sources is empty), revealing name/type/params fields, running the same mandatory probe, then add_source() -- mirroring the CLI wizard's flow and reusing its exact functions. Open question to settle before implementing: how to collect freeform connection params in a TUI -- a multi-line TextArea of key=value pairs mirrors the CLI's own convention and is the simplest match; a fully dynamic per-source-type form is a bigger lift probably not warranted for v1.
close_reason: TUI first-run onboarding: new-source flow, ui starts without config, Home empty-state, run-after-Accept
